title: Leveraging hybrid model of ConvNextBase and LightGBM for early ASD detection via eye-gaze analysis
author: Bidwe, Mishra, et al.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: The proposed work in this paper is an extension of our previous work published in [12]. The previous paper presented two features for identifying Autistic traits: Eye positioning and Attention. This research presents a novel hybrid model of ConvNeXtBase and LightGBM, which successfully predicted autistic traits using eye positioning. ConvNextBase has proven to be the best model in prediction in previous experimentation. This model is tested against traditional successful models like VGG, ResNet, Xception, DenseNet, Inception, and EfficientNet and provides the best prediction accuracy of 78.96 %. Whereas VGG16, VGG19, and EfficientNetB1 were other models that could produce prediction accuracy of 65.37 %, 67.64 %, and 65.21 %, respectively [12]. According to the results, ConvNextBase achieved close to Keras Top 1 Accuracy. The detailed analysis is mentioned below in Table 1.Table 1Performance evaluation of ConvNeXtBase against other pre-trained models.Table 1ModelBestScoreNon-autisticAutisticPrecisionRecallF1-scoreSupportPrecisionRecallF1-scoreSupportVGG160.65370.550.680.613170.750.640.69480VGG190.67640.50.70.582750.80.630.71522ConvNeXtBase0.78960.810.730.774310.720.80.75366EfficientNetB10.65210.820.560.675520.430.730.54245

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: LightGBM is often preferred over XGBoost and CatBoost, considering performance optimization for several reasons given the proposed application. LightGBM is proven to have extremely high speed and efficiency. This is because of the histogram-based algorithm, which reduces computational complexity significantly. This is highly recommended for complex datasets with variable dimensionalities since it reduces time consumption during training without affecting performance. Moreover, LightGBM supports parallel and GPU learning when the data volume is enormous, making it excellent in tasks that require real speed.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: One of the challenges that LightGBM helps to address in the proposed approach is its efficiency in dealing with overfitting. It uses methods such as Leaf-wise (Best-first) tree growth. LightGBM can grow the trees much deeper and far more complex on the most critical splits, achieving finer performance in capturing higher-level patterns in the data. Also, since LightGBM supports categorical features without high preprocessing, this makes the modeling even easier and more robust over a wide range of datasets. Table 2 summarizes empirical evidence regarding the goodness of LightGBM concerning binary classification, surveyed from [42]. It reveals that LightGBM outperforms its competitor models in speed and accuracy over diverse datasets.Table 2Performance analysis of LightGBM against XGBoost and CatBoost.Table 2AlgorithmAccuracyTraining Time in secondsAUC ScoreLightGBM0.8761200.901XGBoost0.8701500.895CatBoost0.8681600.893

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: LightGBM is a gradient-boosting framework that works on the robust foundation of decision trees. This model adapts histogram-based learning, which makes this model fast, highly memory-efficient, and ideal for large and complex data. It groups continuous values in discrete bins, which makes it quicker and saves time. It also uses the leaf-wise tree growth technique, which splits leaves with high and low loss separately, which makes this model more accurate. This makes LightGBM a favorite choice for classification tasks.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: The proposed model uses the expertise of ConvNextBase for feature selection and extraction, followed by LightGBM for classification. The modification made in this hybrid model is discussed in detail later in this section.

steps.title: Methods validation
steps.instruction: To ensure the best possible performance of the proposed model, it is evaluated on several key performance metrics like accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, and specificity. These parameters are evaluated from values interpreted from Fig. 5. These metrics give a full insight into the model's performance at correctly identifying both positive and negative cases, offering a balance between false positives and false negatives, and doing so in a robust generalizing manner. The results of each split and batch size setting are summarized below in tabular form for comparison and further analysis.Fig. 5Interpretation of evaluated metrics after analysis.Fig 5

steps.instruction: Fig. 6 shows the model's performance across different batch sizes before 3-fold cross-validation into three folds to check how changes in batch size would alter the model's capability of learning and generalization. Each batch size addresses important metrics that explain the relationship between batch size and initial performance trends. Fig. 7: Overall performance graph summarizing model results for various batch sizes before running 3-fold cross-validation. It is used to visualize the impact of changing batch size on the general behavior of the model. Table 6 describes the model's performance in certain percentages of accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and specificity. steps.title: Methods validation
steps.instruction: Results in the above table show that the model reaches a peak of 70 % accuracy with a specificity of 78 %. These suggest an encouraging level of performance in terms of the overall capability of the proposed model in making predictions and the rate at which it identifies negative cases correctly. To ensure an in-depth evaluation for better performance of the model, hyperparameter tuning is further performed.

steps.title: Methods validation
steps.instruction: The model was tuned with 3-fold cross-validation. It systematically divides the dataset into three equal parts, with each iteration considering two parts for training and one for testing. Hence, it assures multiple evaluations over different data combinations. Therefore, it also helps mitigate overfitting and ensures the model provides promising results on the complete unseen data.

steps.title: Methods validation
steps.instruction: This cross-validation results in a more reliable estimate of the model's true performance and allows for selecting the most effective hyperparameters.

steps.title: Methods validation
steps.instruction: The model's internal hyperparameters understand the internal training process and adjust values accordingly. Fine-tuning these hyperparameters aims to maximize performance with several metrics. In this process, a grid of random searches is conducted through several possible combinations of provided hyperparameters, with the ultimate objective of finding that combination where the best overall results are obtained. Below, we provide a table of the best hyperparameters tuned with their respective ranges and the final values found to be optimal for this model within the cross-validation. This extra tuning will make the model well-calibrated and most effective at this task.

steps.title: Methods validation
steps.instruction: Some important hyperparameters are chosen and used to maximize the model's performance. ‘Learning_rate’ is used to control the learning speed to converge and adapt to changes in training data. The ‘num_leaves’ sets the maximum number of leaves in a tree, and it would impact on how decision boundaries are defined. ‘max_depth’ restricts the depth of the decision tree. ‘lambda_l1’ and ‘lambda_l2’ represent L1 and L2 regularizations to reduce overfitting. The ‘feature_fraction’ has a value that indicates what fraction of the features are used in every iteration of training; the ‘bagging_fraction’ controls what fraction of the data is used in each iteration of training further for robustification of the model. Table 6 represents a set of provided values and best values used during hyperparameter tuning. It also includes information about improvements in the model because of the best values evaluated. The best parameters were received after comprehensive analysis and 3-fold cross-validation, represented in Table 7.Table 7List of hyperparameters with the provided set of values used during tuning and best values evaluated after tuning.Table 7HyperparameterProvided valuesBest valuesImprovements in proposed system'learning_rate'[0.01, 0.05, 0.1]0.1A learning rate of 0.1 is suited well for the model.'num_leaves'[31, 50, 70],31A maximum of 31 leaves are allowed in a decision tree.'max_depth'[−1, 10, 20],−1This allows unrestricted growth of decision tree'lambda_l1′[0.0, 0.1, 0.5],0.0Features weights are not penalized, and the model performs well during training, so no regularization techniques were used.'lambda_l2′[0.0, 0.1, 0.5],0.0'feature_fraction'[0.8, 0.9, 1.0],0.8Generalization is ensured by using 80 % of the features during each training iteration.'bagging_fraction'[0.8, 0.9, 1.0]0.8Each iteration of the training uses 80 % of the data from the training set to ensure diverse data and prevent overfitting.

steps.title: Methods validation
steps.instruction: The proposed model is re-trained and re-evaluated with the selected best hyperparameters. It is found that the updated models produced better, and more accurate results compared to the previous version, with improved prediction capabilities and generalization. The fundamental metrics values are represented in Table 8. The confusion matrix heatmap is depicted in Fig. 8.Table 8Performance of the proposed hybrid model after providing best values received after hyperparameter tuning.Table 8SplitBatch SizeAccuracyPrecisionRecallF1- ScoreSpecificity90:10160.950.930.840.770.9880:20160.70.70.720.710.6870:30160.740.720.840.760.78Fig. 8Confusion Matrix heatmap of the proposed model after 3—fold cross validation highlighting the model's exceptional capability of classification.Fig 8

steps.title: Methods validation
steps.instruction: Table 7 shows the enhanced performance of the proposed model when evaluated on a dataset with a 90:10 split and a batch size of 16. With this split and batch size, the model can achieve an accuracy of 95 %, which means the proposed model has correctly classified the provided test data. The precision of 93 % indicates that the model had been very effective in minimizing the number of false positives.

steps.title: Methods validation
steps.instruction: Besides, a recall of 84 % suggests that this model was very good at finding true positives, which means it could detect 84 % of all positive cases. Being the balance between precision and recall, the F1 score stands at 0.77, indicating a good trade-off in precision and recall. Finally, a specificity of 98 % denotes the robust capability to identify correctly the negative ones while minimizing the false negatives. These all together indicate the model's performance in class classification.

steps.title: Methods validation
steps.instruction: Additionally, the model yielded a good result with a ROC-AUC score value of 0.91 (Fig. 9), which implies excellent performance in the overall classification. This high value hereby means that the model is really good in relating to the positive and negative classes, such that there is a close-to-perfect balance between sensitivity and specificity at a wide range of thresholds.Fig. 9AUC-ROC curve highlighting classification performance of the proposed model.Fig 9
